name: Fishing (Net and Line)
tagline: Capturing aquatic protein using cordage, hooks, and mesh structures
problem:
Rivers, lakes, and coasts concentrate protein in forms humans cannot outrun or easily spear without tools. Hand-catching works for small shallow species only. The fishing problem is projecting human reach into water: suspending bait at depth, entangling fish in mesh, or gorging swallow hooks — each method trades materials, skill, and bycatch risk.

Fishing also solves seasonality: when terrestrial game is scarce, aquatic resources often remain. Preserved fish (drying, smoking — enabled by cordage racks and fire) becomes a storable amino acid source.

The constraint is tensile reliability underwater: fibres swell, knots slip when wet unless designed correctly, and biofouling weakens lines. Net making is combinatorics: many repeated knots must be uniform or mesh tears propagate.
overview:
Line fishing uses a cord with a terminal hook — historically bone, shell, cactus spine, later metal. Bait or lure triggers swallowing; hook geometry sets jaw penetration angle. Floats and sinkers tune presentation depth using buoyancy and mass along the line.

Nets are textiles in two dimensions: seine nets surround schools; gill nets entangle by mesh size matched to target species; cast nets spread radially from weights at periphery. Knots include sheet bend variants for joining meshes; finger gauges maintain uniform openings.

Traps and weirs channel fish into enclosures — sometimes more fuel-efficient than active netting when streams are reliable. They couple civil engineering (stone walls, woven fences) with ichthyology knowledge.

Ecologically, fishing scales into depletion. Sustainable practice is part of the technology: mesh regulations, seasonal closures, and habitat protection — not optional cultural garnish.

principles.name: Buoyancy and drag
principles.explanation: Floats must support terminal tackle weight; currents add drag that straightens lines and moves baits — design accounts for stream velocity.

principles.name: Mesh selectivity
principles.explanation: Fish attempt passage through openings; correctly sized mesh catches target girth while reducing juvenile take — a mechanical conservation lever.

principles.name: Wet strength of fibres
principles.explanation: Cellulose fibres weaken and swell in water; protein fibres like sinew behave differently. Preservatives (tannins, oils) mitigate rot.

principles.name: Hook mechanics
principles.explanation: Point angle and barb geometry balance penetration versus release; circle hooks reduce deep gut hooking in some fisheries.

components.name: Line / twine
components.function: Transmits setting force and retrieves catch
components.position: Main tensile member from hand or rod to hook
components.madeFrom: Plant cordage, hair, sinew, later silk and nylon
components.criticalNote: Inspect for abrasion before each session

components.name: Hook
components.function: Engages fish mouth or gut depending on design
components.criticalNote: Keep points sharp — dull hooks injure fish without securing catch

components.name: Net panel
components.position: Deployed in water column or across channel
components.criticalNote: Repair tears immediately — propagates under load

components.name: Floats and sinkers
components.madeFrom: Cork, wood, fired clay, stone, lead later
components.warningNote: Lead is toxic — avoid handling residues near food; modern regulations restrict lead tackle in many regions

rawMaterials.name: Cordage fibres
earthLocations: Nettle, lime bast, sisal analogues per biome
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Synthetic monofilament when chemical industry exists
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Spin and ply as rope entry; oil dressing for water resistance

rawMaterials.name: Hook blanks
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Grind point and barb; polish to reduce corrosion pits

rawMaterials.name: Weights
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Shape to reduce snagging on bottom

buildSteps.title: Select target species and method
buildSteps.description: Mesh size, hook size, and bait follow biology — consult local ecological knowledge. Start with abundant small species to calibrate fibres and knots before scaling mesh.
buildSteps.warningNote: Protected species and seasonal closures are legal in most jurisdictions

buildSteps.title: Fabricate hooks and line
buildSteps.description: Carve bone hooks with barb geometry appropriate to jaw strength of target fish. Attach line via snelled whipping or knotted eye. Test dry break strength, then wet soak retest.
buildSteps.warningNote: Bone hooks snap under shock — smooth fighting reduces losses

buildSteps.title: Knit net mesh
buildSteps.description: Tie rows of uniform meshes using a netting needle to shuttle twine. Use gauge stick for opening width. Join panels with strong bends.
buildSteps.warningNote: Uniformity matters — one tight mesh becomes failure nucleus

buildSteps.title: Rig floats and weights
buildSteps.description: For gill nets, space floats on headrope and weights on footrope to achieve vertical hang. Adjust for current — too light rides surface; too heavy snags.
buildSteps.warningNote: Entanglement hazard — never wrap line around limbs

buildSteps.title: Deploy, retrieve, maintain
buildSteps.description: Dry nets after use to prevent rot; remove debris; mend holes. Record catch rates to avoid overharvest.
buildSteps.warningNote: Cold water immersion risk — wear flotation in boats

history.event: Fine barbed bone points and net sinkers proliferate in lacustrine sites.
Fish weirs leave large wooden post patterns in anaerobic sediments.
Commercial production of nylon enables synthetic fishing lines and nets that resist rot — dramatically changing industrial fisheries' economics and ecological impact.

impact:
Fishing supported riverside and coastal sedentism and trade — dried fish is lightweight calories. It also drove boat technologies and navigation later.

Modern industrial fishing is a cautionary lesson: technology without governance collapses stocks. The Codex encodes that governance is a subsystem of extraction tech.
